text: CO2 capture and utilization to chemical commodities such as alcohols is believed to be one of the most efficient strategies to mitigate the release of green house gases. Due to its strong triple bond, the activation of CO2 remains the rate limiting step in catalytic CO2 conversion. Although the bulk gold is inactive to catalytic hydrogenation of CO2, it can be made catalytic when finely dispersed as nanogold particles on certain specific oxide supports. In this study, a synergetic experimental and theoretical investigation was performed to elucidate the catalytic activity of Au nanoparticles dispersed on a specific CeOx/TiO2 catalyst support. It is found that the activation of CO2 occurred at the Au-metal oxide interfacial site, which was induced by electronic polarization and thus served as an active site for CO2 adsorption, leading to high selectivity towards the formation of methanol at low pressure. This work therefore suggests the possibility of tailoring the catalyst activity at the molecular level by adjusting the localized electronic property and structure of the catalyst, which can facilitate the selective catalytic utilization of CO2 towards sustainable fuel production.
